rulings.Case Title: INTERNATIONAL UNION OF OPERATING ENGINEERS VS. SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 30, 2019, Line 2, PETITIONER INTERNATIONAL UNION OF OPERATING ENGINEERS' Hearing On Petition To Compel Arbitration Petitioner International Union of Operating Engineers, Stationary Engineers Local 39, AFL-CIO's petition to compel arbitration is granted. The collective bargaining agreement ("CBA") does not include a clear and unmistakable delegation clause. Therefore, the court rather than the arbitrator will determine whether petitioner "waived" the right to arbitrate by failing to seek arbitration in compliance with the timing requirements of the CBA. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 1281.2(a) [court determines waiver]; Platt Pacific Inc. v. Andelson (1993) 6 Cal.4th 307, 313-314 [court determines whether parties' condition precedent regarding timing was performed to compel arbitration].) In this case, contrary to Respondent San Francisco Unified School District's position, Petitioner did not waive its rights. The CBA provides that the time limits set forth in the grievance and arbitration provision may be extended by mutual agreement. Respondent's letter of September 4, 2018 states that the parties agreed to waive the timing provisions of the CBA. The letter provides: "It should be noted that both sides agreed to waive any timeliness regarding filing and response to the grievance." While Respondent District accuses Petitioner's counsel of lack of candor toward the Court, Respondent omits to mention this explicit agreement by the parties, which is fatal to its waiver argument. The arbitrator shall decide the other merits-based issues that the parties raise, including whether the requested interest is an illegal "penalty." Petitioner has withdrawn its request for an award of attorneys' fees pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 128.7. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for petitioner is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Otis Elevators Compliance With Deposition Subpoena For Business Records; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Otis Elevator Company In The Sum Of 2,750 Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 30, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFFs JUNE CLAUSEN and LARRY CLAUSEN's Motion To Compel Otis Elevators Compliance With Deposition Subpoena For Business Records; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Otis Elevator Company In The Sum Of 2,750 Pro Tem Judge Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is GRANTED. Non-party Otis Elevator is ordered to engage in a diligent search for all responsive documents and produce all such documents within fourteen (14) days. The request for sanctions is GRANTED in the amount of $4,122.00, payable within fourteen (14) days. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOHANNA WELTY VS. CRAIG W. MORRISON
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Johanna Welty'S Motion For Court To Reconsider April 2, 2019 Dismissal (Tentative Ruling Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 30, 2019, Line 10, Plaintiff Johanna Welty'S Motion For Court To Reconsider April 2, 2019 Dismissal (tentative Ruling Only Part 2 Of 2) Even if the Court were to construe the motion for reconsideration as a motion to set aside the dismissal under Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b) (which Plaintiff has not requested it to do), Plaintiff "has failed to identify a colorable basis for relief under the statute." (Hopkins & Carley v. Gens (2011) 200 Cal.App.4th 1401, 1410 [affirming denial of self-represented party's motion to set aside arbitration award against him where he made no real attempt to show it resulted from a mistake of fact or law, surprise, or excusable neglect].) Plaintiff's self-represented status is not grounds for granting such relief. (Burnete v. La Casa Dana Apartments (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 1262, 1267 ["when a litigant accepts the risks of proceeding without counsel, he or she is stuck with the outcome, and has no greater opportunity to cast off an unfavorable judgment than he or she would if represented by counsel."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOHANNA WELTY VS. CRAIG W. MORRISON
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Johanna Welty'S Motion For Court To Reconsider April 2, 2019 Dismissal And Seventh Declaration Of Johanna Welty, Plaintiff Pro Per, Respectfully Submitted To The Superior Court Of California, County Of San Francisco (Case Management Dept 601/ Department 302) In Response To April 2, 2019 Order Granting Motion For Dismissal Filed By Defendant Craig W. Morrison
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 30, 2019, Line 10, Plaintiff Johanna Welty'S Motion For Court To Reconsider April 2, 2019 Dismissal (part 1 of 2) Plaintiff Johanna Welty's motion to reconsider the Court's April 2, 2019 order granting Defendant Craig Morrison's motion to dismiss the action pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 581(f)(2) is denied. Plaintiff filed this action on May 31, 2017. She did not oppose Defendant's demurrer to her original complaint, and failed to comply with the Court's November 6, 2018 order, which gave her 10 days leave to amend. On November 29, 2018, the Court granted Plaintiff a second chance, giving her 60 days leave to file a second amended complaint, due January 31, 2019. Plaintiff failed to comply with that order as well. Further, Plaintiff was given a third and fourth chance: the Court issued orders to show cause on December 26, 2018 and February 21, 2019 directing Plaintiff to show cause why the action should not be dismissed for failure to file a second amended complaint. However, she did not do so at any time before the April 2, 2019 hearing on Defendant's motion to dismiss. She apparently first attempted to file the proposed second amended complaint on May 13, 2019-more than six months after it was originally ordered to be filed, and more than one month after the Court dismissed the action. Plaintiff's voluminous papers offer no explanation for her lengthy delay or her failures to comply with the Court's multiple orders. The most recent physician's letter she has submitted, dated April 16, 2019, offers only an explanation for her not appearing in court in March 2019. Defendant timely filed his motion to dismiss the action on February 15, 2019. Contrary to Plaintiff's assertion that she was not aware of Defendant's motion to dismiss and therefore could not have responded to it, the proof of service and Defendant's counsel's declaration show that Defendant served Plaintiff with the motion on the same date by regular mail at her residence and post office box and by electronic mail. Plaintiff did not file any opposition to that motion, nor did she contest the tentative ruling granting the motion or appear at the April 2, 2019 hearing on the motion. Contrary to her apparent misimpression, Plaintiff is not entitled to a "phone alert" from opposing counsel warning her of upcoming court hearings. Although Plaintiff is self-represented, she has the same obligation as any other party to acquaint herself with and comply with the pertinent provisions of the Code of Civil Procedure and the Court's Local Rules, including her obligations to respond to papers properly filed and served on her, to inform the Court and opposing counsel if she intends to contest a tentative ruling, and to appear at court hearings. (ViaView v. Retzlaff (2016) 1 Cal.App.5th 198, 208 ["While a party may choose to act as her or her own attorney, '[s]uch a party is to be treated like any other party and is entitled to the same, but no greater consideration than other litigants and attorneys. Thus, as is the case with attorneys, [self-represented] litigants must follow correct rules of procedure.'"].) Plaintiff has failed to show any grounds for reconsideration of the Court's order of dismissal. A motion for reconsideration cannot be used to attack an order of dismissal. (APRI Ins. Co. v. Superior Court (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 176.) Further, the motion was not timely filed within 10 days of service of notice of entry of the Court's order, which Defendant served by mail on April 3, 2019. Nor does it establish any new facts, circumstances or law upon which reconsideration may be granted. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MATTHEW DEL VALLE VS. MENZIES AVIATION INC. A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Matthew Del Valle Motion - Notice Of Motion To Approve Paga Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 30, 2019, Line 9, Plaintiff Matthew Del Valle's Motion To Approve Paga Settlement Plaintiff Matthew DelValle's motion for approval of the PAGA settlement agreement, as amended, is granted. The Court finds that the overall amount of the proposed settlement is fair and adequate in view of the purposes and policies of PAGA, taking into account such factors as the adequacy of the civil penalties in light of the potential maximum if the matter were to go to verdict and the risks of litigation. (Lab. Code § 2699(l)(2); see Flores v. Starwood Hotels & Resorts Worldwide, Inc. (C.D. Cal. 2017) 253 F.Supp.3d 1074, 1077; O'Connor v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (N.D. Cal. 2016) 201 F.Supp.3d 1110, 1132-35.) Plaintiff's counsel is awarded $1,612,500 in attorneys' fees and $18,718 in costs, which the Court finds to be a reasonable amount. (Lab. Code §§ 2699(g)(1).) The award represents 30% of the Gross Settlement Amount of $5,375,000, the common fund created by the settlement, an amount that the Court finds to be appropriate under the circumstances of this case. (See Laffitte v. Robert Half Int'l Inc. (2016) 1 Cal.5th 480.) The Court has cross-checked the reasonableness of this fee utilizing the lodestar-multiplier method. Based on the materials submitted with the moving and supplemental papers, the Court finds that Mr. Early's reasonable hourly rate is at least $750 per hour, and that the amount of time spent by counsel on the case was exceedingly reasonable, yielding a total lodestar of at least $358,065. The percentage fee award thus equates to a multiplier of 4.5 or less. Such a multiplier, while high, is appropriate in the circumstances of this case given the contingent risk assumed by counsel, the quality of the representation, and the results obtained, particularly in comparison to those achieved in two class actions raising closely similar claims against one of the same defendants. (Early Decl. 31-35.) Consistent with the settlement agreement, the balance of the $2,150,000 originally requested by counsel as attorneys' fees and costs shall be included in the Net Settlement Amount to be paid to the State of California and the PAGA Class Members. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The Court will sign Plaintiff's proposed stipulated order granting final approval of PAGA settlement and judgment of dismissal, as amended to reflect the Court's rulings and the Amendment to the settlement agreement. In addition, Plaintiff's counsel should be prepared to propose a future compliance date. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GUADALUPE JIMENEZ VS. BRADY CONSTRUCTION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 30, 2019, Line 6, CROSS DEFENDANT AMBER FLOORING, INC.'S DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT Amber Flooring, Inc.'s demurrer to the cross-complaint of Brady Construction, Inc. and the Michael parties is sustained without leave to amend. Labor Code § 3864 provides that: "If an action as provided in this chapter prosecuted by the employee, the employer, or both jointly against the third person results in judgment against such third person, or settlement by such third person, the employer shall have no liability to reimburse or hold such third person harmless on such judgment or settlement in absence of a written agreement so to do executed prior to the injury." This provision generally bars all personal injury and equitable indemnity claims against the employer of the injured employee. (See Alameda Tank Company, Inc. v. Starkist Foods, Inc. (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 428, 433 ["The statutory right of the employer, in situations such as are herein involved, to be free from liability beyond that imposed by the worker's compensation laws cannot be evaded by the ingenuity of counsel in presenting his claim for reimbursement under any variety of theories or nomenclature."].) Brady's assertion that it can plead a claim for express indemnity based on its "usual business relationship" with Amber lacks merit. Brady cannot plead a claim for express indemnity unless it is able to show that prior to the alleged injury, it entered into an express indemnity agreement with respect to the property where the injury occurred. The contract that is (improperly) attached to the opposition was executed after Mr. Jimenez's accident and pertains to a different property. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Amber is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
